statement.markdown: Given the root of a binary tree and a target node value, traverse the tree using in-order traversal and return the first node with the given value. If no such node exists, return null.
statement.inputFormat: The input will be a tree with node values as integers. The target value will be an integer.
statement.outputFormat: The function should return the first node with the target value found during the traversal, or null if no such node exists.
statement.notes: Time complexity should be O(n), where n is the number of nodes in the tree.

examples.input: Example input (matching the stdin format that main wrapper expects)

hints.text: Use in-order traversal to traverse the tree from left to right

editorial.title: Editorial Title
editorial.intuition: The intuition is to traverse the tree using in-order, which visits nodes from left to right. This allows us to find the first occurrence of the target value.
editorial.bruteForce: One possible approach is to traverse the tree using DFS and keep track of the first occurrence of the target value.
editorial.optimalSolution: We can use a recursive DFS approach with a flag to keep track of the first occurrence of the target value.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution can be proved by showing that the in-order traversal visits nodes from left to right.

functionSignature.python: def findTarget(self, root: TreeNode, target: int) -> TreeNode:
